description: Stage 3.1 v2: Export-pipeline optimization on Era 6 stack. Lane B (export-only) plus two budget-attack hypotheses. The export pipeline is worth ~0.01-0.04 BPB combined (quant damage + compression + TTT). TTT is now a default, not a hypothesis.

benchmarks.frontier_pr: PR #1487 (ndokutovich) SP8192 + Recur345 + Par7 + PreQuant TTT 10ep

lane_b_bakeoff.description: Export-only: load external checkpoint (CHECKPOINT_PATH env), apply export patch, TTT, quantize, eval. No training.

decision.description: Full-box eval for promoted export winners.

slots.name: baseline_export_control
slots.why: Lane B control: Era 6 checkpoint, TTT 5ep, standard SDClip GPTQ int6 + int8 embed + brotli. All candidates must beat this.
slots.validates: The export pipeline with TTT reproduces expected deployed score on the Era 6 stack.
slots.falsifies: Lane B comparisons are invalid if this control diverges from expected range.
notes: TTT is now part of the default pipeline, not a hypothesis.

slots.name: fisher_bit_allocation
slots.mechanism: Compute diagonal Fisher per layer from 3 forward-backward passes. Allocate int8 to top-2 highest-Fisher layers, int5 to bottom-2, int6 to rest. Budget-neutral: int5 savings offset int8 cost.
slots.math: F_i = (1/N) sum_n (dL/dW_i)^2 for each layer i. Sort by F_i descending. Top-2: int8 (clip_range=127). Bottom-2: int5 (clip_range=15). Rest: int6 (clip_range=31). Per-row scale s_j = clip_abs_j / clip_range.
slots.placement: After GPTQ Hessian collection, before quantize_int6_gptq. Uses same Hessian model for Fisher computation.
slots.shapes: Fisher per layer: scalar. Bit allocation: per-layer int. Quantization unchanged per-row.
slots.coefficients: K=2 layers int8, K=2 layers int5. 2/11 layers ~18% of model.
slots.failure_modes: Fisher from 3 batches too noisy. Or int5 on low-Fisher layers causes disproportionate damage. Or SDClip already adapts k per-layer well enough.
slots.why: Uniform int6 ignores per-layer sensitivity. Per-layer bit allocation is information-theoretically optimal under a fixed bit budget. Post-TTT, the Fisher landscape may be even more peaked.
slots.kill_rule: No improvement in post_quant_bpb vs R0A_export.
notes: Budget-neutral: int5 saves bytes that offset int8.

slots.name: sdclip_k_sweep
slots.mechanism: Sweep SDClip k per-layer instead of global k=12.85. For each layer, try k in {10.0, 11.5, 12.85, 14.0, 16.0}, pick k that minimizes reconstruction MSE.
slots.math: For layer i: clip_i = k_i * std(W_i, dim=1). Sweep k_i, run GPTQ block per k_i, pick k_i* = argmin MSE_i.
slots.placement: Inside quantize_int6_gptq, replace fixed k with per-layer sweep.
slots.shapes: One scalar k per layer (11 values).
slots.failure_modes: Per-layer k overfits to reconstruction MSE without improving BPB.
slots.why: Different layers have different weight distributions. Post-TTT weights shift further. A global k is a compromise.
slots.kill_rule: Optimal per-layer k is within ±5% of 12.85 for all layers.
notes: Zero cost at inference. Only costs extra time during quantization.

slots.name: ttt_aggressive
slots.mechanism: Push TTT harder: 10 epochs (matching PR #1487), lower lr=0.0003 for fine convergence, freeze only block 0 (unfreeze block 1), add cosine LR schedule within TTT. More params updating + more epochs + scheduled LR = deeper adaptation.
slots.math: TTT loop: for e in 1..10: for batch in D_val: lr_e = 0.0003 * 0.5 * (1 + cos(pi * e / 10)). Update all params except block 0 with AdamW(lr=lr_e, wd=0.01). Only block 0 frozen (vs default freeze blocks 0,1).
slots.placement: Post-training TTT loop. Same location as default TTT but with different hyperparameters.
slots.shapes: Same model, ~9% more unfrozen params (block 1 unfrozen).
slots.init: Start from EMA checkpoint. Fresh AdamW state.
slots.coefficients: epochs=10 (2x default), lr=0.0003 (0.67x default — finer steps with more epochs), freeze=[0] only, cosine schedule within TTT. Rationale: PR #1487 uses 10ep at 0.00045 without cosine. We add cosine to prevent overshooting with the extra unfrozen params.
slots.failure_modes: 10 epochs with more unfrozen params overfits to val data. Or the extra wall time (~20s more) exceeds the export budget. Or unfreezing block 1 destabilizes the learned representations.
slots.why: Default TTT (5ep, freeze 0-1) is conservative. PR #1487 gets -0.034 with 10ep. Our gap is 0.103 BPB — we can't afford to leave TTT BPB on the table. This tests whether more aggressive TTT closes more of the gap.
slots.kill_rule: Worse post_quant_bpb than default TTT (overfit), or exceeds wallclock budget.
notes: Directly attacks the largest known lever.
If this works, it replaces the default TTT config.

slots.name: int4_embed_int6_body
slots.mechanism: Quantize embeddings to int4 instead of int8. Embeddings are 8192×512 = 4M params. At int8 they consume ~4MB (25% of budget). At int4 they consume ~2MB, freeing 2MB for a larger model or higher-precision body weights. This hypothesis tests whether int4 embeddings are viable — if quality holds, the freed budget enables a 13-layer model in stage 3.3.
slots.math: Embed quant: for each row e_i (dim=512), sweep percentiles {0.999, 0.9995, 0.9999, 1.0} for row_clip. scale_i = row_clip / 7, e_q_i = round(e_i / scale_i), clamp to [-7, 7] (symmetric). Stored as int8 (values in [-7,7] range). Dequant: e_i = e_q_i * scale_i. Size savings come from reduced value range improving brotli compression ratio.
slots.placement: In the export quantization step, replace int8 embedding quantization with int4-range values (stored in int8 container).
slots.shapes: Embedding: [8192, 512]. Quantized: [8192, 512] int8 (values in [-7,7]) + [8192] float16 scales.
slots.coefficients: int4 range [-7, 7] symmetric. Per-row scale with percentile sweep.
slots.failure_modes: int4 embeddings lose too much information — SP8192 embeddings need more precision because each token represents a larger text chunk. Or the savings are eaten by scale storage overhead.
slots.why: The 16MB budget is the binding constraint. Embeddings are 25% of the artifact. Halving their size opens the door to a fundamentally larger model. Even if int4 costs 0.003 BPB in quant damage, the freed budget could buy 0.01+ BPB of model capacity.
slots.kill_rule: int4 embed quant damage exceeds 0.005 BPB with no model-size offset.
notes: This is a budget enabler, not a standalone win.
If viable, enables 13L model in stage 3.3 (H3).

slots.name: gptq_post_ttt
slots.mechanism: Activation-weighted quantization: run calibration batches through the model post-TTT, collect E[|x|] per CastedLinear input feature. During quantization, weight the reconstruction MSE by activation magnitude so that columns seeing larger inputs get more careful clipping. This approximates the diagonal of the Fisher/Hessian without computing actual second-order terms.
slots.math: For each CastedLinear with weight W [out, in]: collect a_j = E[|x_j|] over 32 calibration sequences. Normalize: aw_j = a_j / mean(a). During quant search: err = mean((W - W_q)^2 * aw[None, :]) instead of plain MSE. Columns with high activation get higher weight in the error metric.
slots.placement: Before mixed_quantize_int6: register forward hooks on all CastedLinear modules, run 4 calibration batches, store per-layer activation weights in _ACT_WEIGHT_MAP. The quantizer reads _CURRENT_QUANT_NAME to look up weights.
slots.shapes: Activation weight per layer: [in_features] float32. Same quantization shapes as baseline.
slots.coefficients: 32 calibration sequences in batches of 8. Activation weights normalized to mean=1.
slots.failure_modes: Activation magnitudes are roughly uniform across features (no useful signal). Or the activation-weighted error metric disagrees with the loss-optimal clipping.
slots.why: Plain MSE treats all weight columns equally, but columns multiplied by large activations contribute more to output error. Activation-weighting is a cheap first-order proxy for Fisher-optimal quantization.
notes: Adds ~2s calibration overhead, zero inference cost.
Activation-weighted MSE is a cheap proxy for Fisher-optimal quantization.
Works on post-TTT weights if TTT ran first.
